text: This video describes a safe and fast way of preparing the regius cavity and ventral aspect of the prostate during robot-assisted laparoscopic radical prostatectomy. It is not only suitable for inexperienced consult surgeons, but also for experienced ones.

The surgeon uses a monopolar scissor in the right hand and a PK dissector for tissue preparation, while employing bipolar coagulation in the left hand. A Prograsp instrument is used for retraction with the third robotic arm.

The surgeon begins preparation in the midline near the umbilicus, incising the peritoneum and releasing the bladder from the ventral abdominal wall. The dissection continues towards the pubic bone and symphysis, using a monopolar scissor and PK dissector to pull the bladder down and identify the pubic bone, symphysis, and ventral aspect of the prostate. The robotic instruments create an extraperitoneal space, resembling the inflation of a balloon trocar.

The surgeon identifies the lateral limit of the preparation at the junction between the ligamenta umbilicale medial and the vas deferens, crossing above the iliac artery. The peritoneum is pulled contralaterally with the Prograsp, while the monopolar scissor and PK dissector are used in a ventral fashion to reveal the iliac vein, obturator nerve, and endopelvic fascia.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection on the tissue located between the lateral and dorsal aspects of the prostate, repeating the procedure from the contralateral side.

This technique has been performed in more than five hundred cases, it lasts about four to five minutes, no complications have been observed, it provides maximal exposure of the surgical area, in cases of lymph node dissection it prepares the area and mostly it's simple and easy to be learned from an inexperienced consult surgeon.
